coredata.dc:title: Ultrasonic synthesis of high fluorescent C-dots and modified with CuWO4 nanocomposite for effective photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: ReddyPrasad, Puthalapattu
Naidoo, Eliazer Bobby
coredata.dc:description: High fluorescent C-dots were synthesized from dextrose via facile ultrasonic wave assisted reaction. Carbon dots (C-dots)/Copper tungstate (CuWO4) heterostructure has been prepared via a facile reflux approach. The novel C-dots/CuWO4 photocatalyst were characterized by using transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–vis, photoluminescence and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The photocatalytic property of heterostructure nanocomposite has also been investigated by using rhodamine B as a model organic pollutant. The obtained results revealed that as prepared C-dots are predominately multilayer graphene oxide with luminance properties. After incorporation of C-dots onto the CuWO4, it contributes to the improvement of charge separation and reduction of charge recombination and thereby enhancing the photocatalytic property, respectively. The C-dots in the nanocomposites can efficiently trap electrons, thus hindering the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The heterostructure nanocomposite with 5.0 wt% C-dots shows the highest photocatalytic activity, which is about three times as that of pure CuWO4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuWO4
C-dots
Rhodamine B
Photocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of C-dots, (B) average particle size of C-dots.

UV–vis absorption (black curve) and PL emission (blue curve) spectra of the aqueous dispersion of the dots (λexi = 360 nm). Inset: the photograph of C-dots dispersion under UV light (λexi = 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
C-Dots (A) C1s (B) O1s (C) XPS of C-dots.
XRD spectrum of CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposite.
TEM images of CuWO4 and C-dots modified CuWO4.
FTIR spectrum of CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposite.
UV–vis absorption spectra of CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 (A) PL spectrum of CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposite (B).
Absorption spectra changes of C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposite (A), kinetics curves of the photocatalytic degradation of RhB (B).
Effect of solution pH (A) and percentage of RhB degraded for each cycle (B).
Schematic diagram of novel photocatalyst and photoexcited electron–hole separation process.

head.ce:title: Ultrasonic synthesis of high fluorescent C-dots and modified with CuWO4 nanocomposite for effective photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Vaal University of Technology, P. Bag X021, Vanderbijlpark 1900, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High fluorescent C-dots were synthesized from dextrose via facile ultrasonic wave assisted reaction. Carbon dots (C-dots)/Copper tungstate (CuWO4) heterostructure has been prepared via a facile reflux approach. The novel C-dots/CuWO4 photocatalyst were characterized by using transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–vis, photoluminescence and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The photocatalytic property of heterostructure nanocomposite has also been investigated by using rhodamine B as a model organic pollutant. The obtained results revealed that as prepared C-dots are predominately multilayer graphene oxide with luminance properties. After incorporation of C-dots onto the CuWO4, it contributes to the improvement of charge separation and reduction of charge recombination and thereby enhancing the photocatalytic property, respectively. The C-dots in the nanocomposites can efficiently trap electrons, thus hindering the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The heterostructure nanocomposite with 5.0 wt% C-dots shows the highest photocatalytic activity, which is about three times as that of pure CuWO4.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution has become the biggest problem for the human race on this planet. Most of the pollutants come from industrial run-off and chemical fertilizers used in farming that end up in the water bodies [1]. Every year 280,000 tons of synthetic dyes have been estimated to be released into the environment on the planet through industrial wastewater discharges [2]. Dyes are tinctorially strong, hence easily visible in contaminated waters even at very low concentrations. It's also often highly toxic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic in nature, and can even bio-accumulate in the food chain [3]. Recently, heterogeneous photocatalysis has been an advanced oxidation technology (AOT) that can be applied to solve environmental and energy problems [4,5]. Heterogeneous photocatalysis involves the utilization of a semiconductor catalyst (such as TiO2, ZnO, Fe2O3, CdS, or ZnS), irradiated with light of an appropriate wavelength, to generate highly reactive transitory species (i.e., •OH, •O2 −, •HO2) for mineralization of organic impurities [6–9]. This green technology offers a number of advantages such as ambient operating conditions, low operating costs, complete mineralization of organic pollutant without any secondary pollution, all of which have promoted its wide application in wastewater treatment [10]. Unfortunately, the insufficient quantum efficiency, narrow excitation wavelength, high recombination rate of the photoproduced electron–hole pairs, contributes to poor adsorption capacity and deactivation of the semiconductor photocatalyst [11,12]. Therefore, several methods have been reported to reduce the recombination of charge carriers by coupling the photocatalyst with electron scavenging agents, such as noble metals [13], including metal particle loading [14], co-catalysts [15], dyesensitization [16], metallic and non-metallic doping [17], semiconductors [18] and carbon nanomaterials [19–21].

More than ever, carbon nanomaterials have attracted much attention in photocatalysis ﬁeld due to their excellent electron transfer ability, good stability and dispensability, low cost and cytotoxicity [22]. Now, many efforts have been made on the preparation of photocatalyst composite using C60 [23], graphene [24] and carbon nanoﬁber [25] as electron scavenging agents [26], but rarely involved carbon dots [27]. Compared to other carbon nanomaterials, C-dots have lower cost and better solubility in water and wider preparation methods. In-addition, nanostructured tungstate materials have stimulated intensive research interest because of their suitable band gap, sufficient chemical and thermal stability, adaptability to various detectors, laser hosts, photoanodes, optical fibers, as well as more stable against photocorrosion in aqueous solution at neutral pH [28–30]. Therefore, it is expected that utilizing the above excellent properties of C-dots and CuWO4 to prepare C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure will offer a new opportunity for the improvement of charge separation and the reduction of charge recombination, thus enhancing of photocatalytic efficiency.

Herein, we report the facile ultrasonic synthesis of high fluorescent C-dots and the C-dots modified CuWO4 nanocomposite which was prepared via facile reflux method. The as-prepared photocatalyst was examined by characterizing the structure, morphology and optical properties. The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite (C-dots, CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4) towards the degradation of rhodamine B was investigated. The C-dots/CuWO4 photocatalyst efficiently utilize the UV light and greatly enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Dextrose (99+%) and NaOH was purchased from Across Organic. CuCl2·5H2O, Na2WO4·2H2O and phosphate buffer were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Na2CO3, H2SO4, HNO3 and rhodamine B (RhB) were of analytical grade and used without any further puriﬁcation. Deionized water was used in all preparations. All other supplied chemicals were of standard reagent grade quality.

Measurements of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were carried out with a Shimadzu UV-2200 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer by a KBr pellet method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were achieved on JEM-100CXG and Philips Tecnai F20 electron microscope. The crystal structure of the resultant products was characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) by using X'Pert-Pro MPD (Holland) D/max-γAX-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on an ESCALAB MK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. All the measurements were carried out at room temperature (∼25 °C).

A suitable amount of dextrose was dissolved in ultrapure water (50 mL) to form a clear solution (1 mol L−1). 4.0 g of NaOH (50 mL) solution was added to the solution of dextrose, then the mixed solution was treated by an ultrasonic wave (150 W, 40 KHz) for 60 min. The unfinished sample obtained from dextrose/NaOH was adjusted to pH 7.0 with 1 mol/L hydrochloric acid. Subsequently, the carbogenic precipitate was then separated by centrifugation, and the C-dots were collected from the supernatant by dialysis for 24 h.

CuWO4 was prepared by the co-precipitation method. In a typical procedure equimolar quantities of copper sulphate (CuSO4·5H2O) and sodium tungstate (Na2WO4·2H2O) were separately dissolved in double distilled water. After that, the sodium tungstate solution was added drop wise into the copper sulphate solution under constant stirring. Subsequently the pH of the solution was adjusted to 8.0 using 0.1 M NaOH and the whole mixture was stirred at room temperature for 5 h. The precipitate obtained was washed with double distilled water several times and dried at 100 °C for 2 h followed by calcination at 450 °C for 6 h.

To prepare C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposite, 0.5 g of CuWO4 was added to 0.015 g of C-dots in aqueous solution. The nanocomposite was obtained via an oil bath reflux strategy at 250 °C for 4 h. The obtained C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposite was washed with ultrapure water and finally dried in an oven at 200 °C for 6 h.

The photocatalytic experiments investigated the UV assisted degradation of RhB in aqueous solution, at room temperature, over synthesized C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposite using a 110 mV/cm2 mercury lamp as the light source (λ < 420 nm). All reactions were carried out in a magnetically stirred glass vessel, open at the top, located at a distance of 10 cm under UV lamp. The ratio of photocatalyst (10 mg of catalyst/50 ml of RhB solution) was constantly maintained, during all photocatalytic tests and the initial concentration of the dye in aqueous solution at pH 7.0. In order to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium, all solutions were magnetically stirred for 30 min in complete darkness, then irradiated in the same conditions in order to avoid the influence of the outer light. The concentration of RhB in all solutions was monitored from 300 to 700 nm with a Schimadzu 2200 UV–vis spectrophotometry.

In our experiment, a facile and one-step ultrasonic synthetic itinerary was employed for the synthesis of C-dots from dextrose. The morphology and structure of graphitic like C-dots were confirmed by UV–vis, Fluorescence, Raman spectroscopy, XPS and TEM analysis. Fig. 1 A, shows the TEM images of the C-dots, which can be seen to have a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation and the corresponding C-dots size distribution histogram obtained indicates that these dots have size ∼3.2 nm (Fig. 1B). Fig. 2 , shows the UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of the aqueous dispersion of the C-dots. The UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots in water shows an absorption band at 340 nm attributed to the Л–Л* transition. The C-dots dispersion shows a strong PL emission peak centered at 448 nm when excited at 360 nm, indicating that the C-dots are high fluorescent. Inserts of Fig. 2, shows the photograph of the water-dispersed C-dots illuminated under 365 nm UV light. It shows bluish green fluorescence color can be observed by exposed eye, which is similar to the graphene quantum dots and probably attributable to their small size and surface states [31]. Fig. 3 , shows the Raman spectra of C-dots of two characteristic peaks observed at 1360 and 1580 cm− 1 corresponding to the D-band and G-band, which is similar to the graphene structure. The above observations confirmed that the formation of graphitic like C-dots from dextrose. The surface composition of the synthesized C-dots was characterized by the XPS technique. In Fig. 4 A, the XPS spectrum of the C-dots shows two peaks at 284.8 and 531.7 eV which can be attributed to C1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4B) shows three peaks at 284.6, 285.2, and 287.8 eV, which are attributed to C–C, CO, and C–O, respectively. While the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4C) shows two peaks at 530.4 and 532.6 eV, which are attributed to the CO and C–OH/C–O–C bands, respectively. The above observations confirm that the synthesized CN-dots functioned with hydroxyl, carboxylic and carbonyl moieties.

Primary analysis of photocatalytic degradation of model organic pollutant of RhB with different C-dots loadings on CuWO4 catalysts was carried out. Pseudo-first order rate constants determined for 3.0, 5.0 and 7.0 wt% C-dots loading were 0.44, 0.49 and 0.40 min−1, respectively. Upon this 5.0 wt% of C-dots were found to be the most efficient. Hence, 5.0 wt% of C-dots was taken as optimum loading of C-dots on CuWO4 and this catalyst was characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, PL and DRS respectively. The formation of transition metal tungstate X-ray diffraction patterns of sample synthesized by the co-precipitation method at 400 °C for 6 h was measured, as shown in Fig. 5 . The following results permit to conclude that the experimental conditions used were favorable for the formation of the fine CuWO4. For the CuWO4 sample, the characteristic diffraction peaks of CuWO4 are corresponding well to typical CuWO4 phase (Powder diffraction file No. 731823) [32], suggesting a spherical like CuWO4 crystal was synthesized after the annealing process. While for the C-dots/CuWO4 sample, a new diffraction peak centered at 26.02° attributing to (0 0 2) peak of graphite evidently appears, indicating that the C-dots were successfully coated on CuWO4 layer as similar carbon coated semiconductor composites [31]. The TEM images of synthesized CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 are shown in Fig. 6 A and B, respectively. The TEM images of prepared CuWO4 (Fig. 6A) shows that the nanoparticles are in homogeneous morphologies (roughly spherical), including some isolated nanoparticles sharp edges. Fig. 6B reveals the presence of crystalline and well defined particles with carbon as dark spots on CuWO4. The sizes of these particles are in the range of 18–25 nm in both catalysts, which is consistent with the average crystallite size of prepared CuWO4 (20.5 nm) and C-dots loaded CuWO4 (19.2 nm) estimated from XRD analysis [33].

The surface changes of CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 were further analyzed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the strong peaks of sample at 500, 748 and 876 cm−1 are attributed to M−O stretching vibrations [33] and the absorption bands observed at around of 1600 and 3400 cm−1 of C-dots/CuWO4 sample to the characteristic peaks of CO and O–H stretching vibrations. Notably, it can be seen that an extra peaks at 2940, 1680, 1400 cm−1 which can be attributed to aromatic –C–H, CO and C–H after the addition of C-dots. Furthermore, compared with the FT-IR spectra of C-dots/CuWO4 and pure CuWO4 sample (Fig. 7b), the CO and O–H stretching, absorption peak intensities are clearly weaker. From these results, it can be deduced that the C-dots because of their small size, low amount and almost functional groups located on the edges of the surface.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared CuWO4 nanoparticles and C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposites were recorded, as shown in Fig. 8 A. Both of the two samples exhibit a representative absorption with intense transition in the UV–visible region due to the bandgap transition of CuWO4 semiconductor. Loading of C-dots on CuWO4 caused a slight shift in the absorption band from 395 to 407 nm and it can be interpreted as possible evidence for favorable interaction between CuWO4 and C-dots. The band gap energies of CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 were found to be 2.95 and 2.74 eV, respectively. Specifically, the C-dots loading on CuWO4 decreases the band gap energy of CuWO4. Significantly, the result reveals that the C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposites may exhibit a higher photocatalytic activity than that of pure CuWO4 nanoparticles for target reactions under UV light.

The efficiency of charge trapping and recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes in the semiconductor can be verified by PL spectrum. Fig. 8B shows the PL spectra of the CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposites. The result confirms highly efficient interfacial charge transfer between C-dots and CuWO4. The C-dots on the CuWO4 photocatalyst system effectively afford excitation separation interfaces and carrier transporting pathways, leading to the dramatic decrease of PL intensity. The C-dots also provide a facile way to direct the flow of photogenerated charge carriers, which leads to the enhancement of photocatalytic performance of the C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposites.

Photocatalytic activity is strongly dependent on the transportation and separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes in the catalysts. In order to investigate the UV light photocatalytic performance of the C-dots/CuWO4 samples toward the degradation, RhB was selected as a model contaminant for photocatalytic reaction. The solution was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to equilibrate the adsorption and desorption process prior to the irradiation. Fig. 9 A shows the change in the absorption spectra of RhB aqueous solution which was exposed to UV light in the presence of CuWO4 and C-dots/CuWO4 samples. After irradiation of UV light for 1.2 h, the degradation rate efficiency of RhB was found to be about 29.67% and 88.09% (Fig. 9B) with the assistance of CuWO4 and 5.0 wt% C-dots/CuWO4 samples, respectively. Obviously, the C-dots in the C-dots/CuWO4 composites are beneficial for the degradation of RhB. When C-dots are attached to the CuWO4, the matched band edge permits the electrons to transfer from CuWO4 surface to C-dots, which is favorable for the charge separation and stabilization [34].

The higher photocatalytic activity of C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure than CuWO4 sample indicates that the surface interaction between CuWO4 and C-dots are important, which can signiﬁcantly increase the separation of photogenerated charge and enhance the transfer of electron–hole pairs. The improvement of photocatalytic activity of CuWO4 over C-dots coated can be attributed to the efﬁcient charge separation caused by the rapid photoinduced charge transfer [34] and the inhibition of the recombination of electron–hole pairs as conﬁrmed by PL spectroscopy. The efﬁcient charge separation can increase the lifetime of the charge carriers and the inhibition of the recombination of electron–hole pairs will enhance the efﬁciency of the interfacial charge transfer to adsorbed substrates, and thus increase the number of carriers adsorbed by contaminant.

Based on above experimental results, we can conclude that C-dots must play critical roles for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of C-dots/CuWO4 nanocomposites. When the C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure is irradiated by UV light with the energy higher band-gap of CuWO4, the electrons (e−) in the valence band of CuWO4 can be excited into the conduction band and followed by moving freely to the surface of C-dots through the photoinduced charge transfer process meanwhile, the holes (h+) leave from the valence band of CuWO4. Subsequent the holes (h+) to produce •OH from the surface of H2O or OH−. The photo-generated electrons can react with the dissolved oxygen on the surface of C-dots to form radicals •O2−, which cause degradation of RhB [35]. The schematic illustration of the mechanism for the photocatalysis of the C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure is summarized in Scheme 1 . This phenomenon also shows that C-dots indeed play an important role in the higher degradation efficiency of RhB at the room temperature.

The influence of pH is most important parameter for degradation of the RhB. The effect of pH on the photodegradation of RhB was studied in the pH range 3.0–11(Fig. 10 A). The effect of pH on degradation of pollutants is variable and controversial since the positive holes are considered as the major oxidation species at low pH, whereas hydroxyl radicals are considered as the predominant species at high pH levels. Since the influence of pH depends on the type of dye and on properties of photocatalyst surface, its effect on the photocatalytic efficiency must be accurately checked before any application. Therefore, the initial pH is adjusted by adding 1 N HCl or I N NaOH. The highest efficiency for the degradation rate of RhB is observed at pH 7, where 88.09% of RhB is decomposed after 120 min in the presence of C-dot/CuWO4 at room temperature. After that, the degradation rate for the RhB decreases with the increase in reaction pH up to 8 due to nucleophilic hydroxyl ions attack of the dye. It is found that the optimum pH was 7.0 for efficient RhB degradation on 5.0 wt% C-dots/CuWO4. In addition to photocatalytic activity, the stability of the photocatalysts is another important consideration for practical applications. The stability of the C-dots/CuWO4 for recycling was also studied and the result is illustrated in Fig. 10B. The stability of the 5.0 wt% C-dots/CuWO4 was examined for degradation of RhB for four cycles, which still revealed high degradation efficiency, indicating that the C-dots/CuWO4 composites are reproducible catalysts. The C-dots/CuWO4 composites settled down in the dispersion after natural settlement for 5 min, which indicates that the CuWO4 spheres can be easily separated for recycle.

In summary, we afford a facile ultrasonic method to obtain a high fluorescence graphitic like C-dots and prepare a C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure via a reflux approach process. The heterostructure of C-dots/CuWO4 can signiﬁcantly increase the photocatalytic efﬁciency of C-dots. The degree of photocatalytic activity enhancement strongly depended on the amount of C-dots nanoparticles, while the sample of C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure with 5.0 wt% of C-dots show the highest photocatalytic activity. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure is attributed to the efﬁcient charge separation and the inhibition of the recombination of electron–hole pairs due to the electronic interaction between the C-dots and CuWO4. The C-dots/CuWO4 heterostructure photocatalyst was found to be stable and reusable.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author appreciates the ﬁnancial support from Vaal University of Technology, South Africa, for postdoctoral research fellowship.
